input.task: What is a frame in Smithers and what enables the runtime to support time travel, observability, hot reload, and resume based on the frame?

input.task: State the durability contract for Smithers resumption. What happens to completed tasks and in-flight tasks when a run is resumed after a crash?

verify.rubric: A correct answer should convey: A completed task is never re-executed. Resume loads persisted state, cleans stale in-progress attempts (>15 min without a heartbeat are abandoned), re-renders, and continues.

input.task: How does hot reload differ from resume? Explain why you can edit code during a run with hot reload but not across resume.

verify.rubric: A correct answer should convey: Hot reload applies code changes to newly scheduled tasks while in-flight tasks finish on their original code. Resume validates the workflow source hash, so a source change blocks resume.

input.task: Why must task IDs be stable and data-derived for resume to work correctly?

verify.rubric: A correct answer should convey: Task IDs must be stable across renders. A changed task ID looks like a new task to the runtime, and an old task whose ID disappeared is dropped from the plan. Derive from data, not indices or timestamps.

input.task: Explain what fork does, why it is immutable, and why multiple tasks can safely fork the same source in parallel.

verify.rubric: A correct answer should convey: fork copies the source conversation into a fresh, independent session and submits the new prompt. The source is never mutated, so many tasks can fork the same source in parallel and a forked task can itself be forked.

input.task: What happens when a ContinueAsNew is rendered? How is state carried to the next run?

What is the difference between ctx.output(nodeId), ctx.latest(nodeId), and ctx.outputs.<schemaName> when reading task results in a Smithers workflow?

verify.rubric: ctx.output/ctx.latest read a single node's output; ctx.outputs.<schemaName> is the FULL array of every row written for that schema across all nodes and loop iterations (used for per-item fan-out filtering). Must capture that ctx.outputs.<schema> is an array across nodes/iterations.

input.task: In a Smithers workflow render, my ctx.input.maxConcurrency is null even though the Zod input schema has .default(4). Why, and what's the fix?

verify.rubric: ctx.input fields arrive as their raw value or null, NOT the Zod default; always coalesce (ctx.input?.maxConcurrency ?? 4). Must identify that defaults are not applied to ctx.input and coalescing is the fix.

input.task: How do I drop down to Smithers' lower-level Effect-ts API to define a durable step imperatively (instead of JSX)? Name the entry API.

verify.rubric: Smithers.workflow().step(...) exposes the raw Effect-ts surface (Schedules, Layers, fibers) and can be mixed with JSX. A correct answer names Smithers.workflow().step().

verify.rubric: Correct answer must explain that skip-remaining skips the current task AND the rest of the tasks in the Aspects scope. From 0.24.1 changelog: 'skip-remaining skips the task and the rest of the scope.' skip-remaining is only available for tokenBudget, not latencySlo.

input.task: How do nested <Aspects> components inherit budget configuration from parent scopes? Is it a per-field merge or wholesale replacement?

verify.rubric: Correct answer: wholesale replacement per config field (tokenBudget, latencySlo) — if inner Aspects sets tokenBudget, the entire parent tokenBudget config is replaced with no per-field merge. Exception: tracking config does per-field fallback. From /docs/components/aspects.mdx: 'If an inner <Aspects> sets tokenBudget or latencySlo, that field's entire parent config object is replaced (no per-field merge). The tracking config is the exception: tokens and latency each fall back to the parent value independently.'

input.task: Does the tracking configuration affect whether budgets are enforced?

verify.rubric: Correct answer: No, tracking does not affect enforcement. From /docs/components/aspects.mdx: 'Budgets enforce regardless of tracking; tracking controls metric emission only.'

input.task: How does token usage accumulation behave when a run is resumed after being paused or interrupted?

verify.rubric: Correct answer must explain that accumulated token usage survives resume, and is re-seeded from persisted TokenUsageReported events. From 0.24.1 changelog: 'Accumulated token usage survives resume (re-seeded from persisted token-usage events).' and /docs/components/aspects.mdx: 'Accumulated token usage is per run and survives resume (re-seeded from persisted token-usage events).'
